hook: You already know that some numbers divide evenly into others — like how 6 splits cleanly into 12, but not into 13. What you might not know is that this simple idea of divisibility is the hidden engine behind internet security, encrypted passwords, and the codes that protect your bank account. In this unit, you'll discover how the humble integers hold secrets powerful enough to secure the modern world.
concept: Number theory is the study of the deep structure hiding inside the ordinary integers — how they divide, factor, and relate to one another — unified by the formal framework of modular arithmetic and divisibility.

topic_roadmap.description: You will learn to formally define divisibility and use the Division Algorithm to express any integer uniquely as a quotient and remainder, building the foundation for everything that follows.

topic_roadmap.topic_name: GCD, LCM, and the Euclidean Algorithm
topic_roadmap.description: Master the Euclidean Algorithm to efficiently compute the greatest common divisor $\gcd(a, b)$ and least common multiple of two integers, even when the numbers are extremely large.

topic_roadmap.topic_name: Primes and the Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic
topic_roadmap.description: Apply the Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic to prove that every integer greater than 1 has a unique prime factorization, and explore why primes are the indivisible building blocks of all integers.
You will learn to work with congruences $a \equiv b \pmod{n}$ and perform arithmetic on remainders, unlocking a powerful system that makes large-number calculations manageable.
You will solve equations of the form $ax \equiv b \pmod{n}$, determining when solutions exist and finding them systematically using the tools developed in earlier topics.
Apply the Chinese Remainder Theorem to reconstruct a number from its remainders modulo several pairwise coprime integers, solving systems of congruences with elegance and efficiency.

topic_roadmap.topic_name: Fermat's Little Theorem and Euler's Theorem (H)
topic_roadmap.description: You will use Fermat's Little Theorem and Euler's Theorem to dramatically simplify computations like $a^{1000} \pmod{n}$, results that are essential to modern cryptographic algorithms.

You will see how all the number theory from this unit comes together in the RSA cryptosystem, understanding exactly why encrypting and decrypting messages with public and private keys is both practical and mathematically secure.
